item.title: “Monks, form is suffering. What is suffering is not-self. What is not-self should be seen as it really is with correct wisdom: ‘This is not…
item.description:
“Monks, form is suffering. What is suffering is not-self. What is not-self should be seen as it really is with correct wisdom: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ Feeling … Perception … Formations … Consciousness is suffering. What is suffering is not-self. What is not-self should be seen as it really is with correct wisdom: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ Seeing thus, monks, a well-taught noble disciple becomes disenchanted with form … disenchanted with feeling … disenchanted with perception … disenchanted with formations … disenchanted with consciousness. Being disenchanted, he becomes dispassionate. Through dispassion, he is liberated … ‘… there is no more of this state of being.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.16
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And how, Mahānāma, does a noble disciple guard the sense faculties? Here, Mahānāma, when a noble disciple sees a form with the eye, he does…
item.description:
“And how, Mahānāma, does a noble disciple guard the sense faculties? Here, Mahānāma, when a noble disciple sees a form with the eye, he does not grasp the general feature, does not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the eye faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—he restrains that cause, guards the eye, practices restraint of the eye. When the ear hears a sound … nose smells an odor … tongue tastes a flavor … body feels a touch … mind cognizes phenomena, he does not grasp the general feature, does not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the mind faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—he restrains that cause, guards the mind, practices restraint of the mind. Thus, Mahānāma, a noble disciple guards the sense faculties.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 53 – Sekhasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, what is Right Effort? Monks, here a monk awakens desire for the non-arising of unarisen evil, unwholesome states; he makes an effort, arouses energy,…
item.description:
“Monks, what is Right Effort? Monks, here a monk awakens desire for the non-arising of unarisen evil, unwholesome states; he makes an effort, arouses energy, applies his mind, and strives. He awakens desire for the abandoning of arisen evil, unwholesome states; he makes an effort, arouses energy, applies his mind, and strives. He awakens desire for the arising of unarisen wholesome states; he makes an effort, arouses energy, applies his mind, and strives. He awakens desire for the continuance, non-disappearance, strengthening, increase, and fulfillment of arisen wholesome states; he makes an effort, arouses energy, applies his mind, and strives. This, monks, is called Right Effort.”

From: Dīgha Nikāya 22 – Mahāsatipaṭṭhānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Like a great rocky mountain, Standing up to the sky, Advancing and surrounding, Crushing everything on all sides — So too, aging and death Advance…
item.description:
Like a great rocky mountain,
Standing up to the sky,
Advancing and surrounding,
Crushing everything on all sides —
So too, aging and death
Advance upon beings.
Warriors, brahmins, merchants,
Workers, outcasts,
Those who collect rubbish and feces —
No one can escape.
All are conquered.
No elephant corps,
No chariot corps, no infantry,
No battle of spells,
No battle of wealth
Can help one win
Against aging and death.
Therefore, the wise person,
Seeing what is good for oneself,
Places faith
In the Buddha, Dhamma, and Saṅgha.
Whoever by body, speech, and mind
Practices in accordance with the Dhamma,
Is praised in this life
And in the next enjoys heavenly bliss.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 3.25
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Along with this suffering, abandon sensual desires, Seeking future security from bondage. Rightly understanding, with mind well liberated, There, right there, one is liberated from…
item.description:
“Along with this suffering, abandon sensual desires,
Seeking future security from bondage.
Rightly understanding, with mind well liberated,
There, right there, one is liberated from contact.
That one attains knowledge,
Accomplishes the holy life,
Reaches the end of the world,
Gets to the far shore —
That one is so called.”

From: Itivuttaka 109
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, whatever is impermanent, you should abandon your desire for it. And what, monks, is impermanent? Form, monks, is impermanent … Feeling … Perception ……
item.description:
“Monks, whatever is impermanent, you should abandon your desire for it. And what, monks, is impermanent? Form, monks, is impermanent … Feeling … Perception … Formations … Consciousness is impermanent — there you should abandon your desire. Whatever is impermanent, monks, there you should abandon your desire.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.137
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And what, monks, is the noble search? Here, monks, a person being himself subject to birth, having known the peril in what is subject to…
item.description:
“And what, monks, is the noble search? Here, monks, a person being himself subject to birth, having known the peril in what is subject to birth, seeks the unborn, supreme security from bondage, Nibbāna; being himself subject to aging, having known the peril in aging, seeks the unaging, supreme security from bondage, Nibbāna; being subject to sickness … the unailing … subject to death … the deathless … subject to sorrow … the sorrowless … subject to defilement, having known the peril in defilement, seeks the undefiled, supreme security from bondage, Nibbāna. This, monks, is called the noble search.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 26 – Ariyapariyesanāsutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And what, monks, is the practice of calming? Here, monks, a monk does not consent to arisen sensual thought; he abandons, dispels, calms, ends, and…
item.description:
“And what, monks, is the practice of calming? Here, monks, a monk does not consent to arisen sensual thought; he abandons, dispels, calms, ends, and makes it non-existent. He does not consent to arisen ill-will thought … harmful thought … to arisen evil, unwholesome thoughts; he abandons, dispels, calms, ends, and makes them non-existent. This, monks, is called the practice of calming.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 4.164
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - “Friend, I do not abandon the present to chase after things subject to time. But, friend, I abandon things subject to time to chase…
item.description:
- “Friend, I do not abandon the present to chase after things subject to time. But, friend, I abandon things subject to time to chase after the present. The Blessed One has said that sensual pleasures are subject to time, of great suffering, of great despair — the peril in them is even greater. This Dhamma is visible here and now, not subject to time, inviting inspection, leading onward, to be directly experienced by the wise.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 1.20
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And how, monks, is a monk one who has completely cut off all searches? Here, monks, a monk has abandoned the search for sensual pleasures,…
item.description:
“And how, monks, is a monk one who has completely cut off all searches? Here, monks, a monk has abandoned the search for sensual pleasures, abandoned the search for existence, and the search for the holy life has been calmed. Thus, monks, is a monk who has completely cut off all searches. And how, monks, is a monk one whose thoughts are unblemished? Here, monks, a monk has abandoned sensual thought, abandoned ill-will thought, abandoned harmful thought. Thus, monks, is a monk whose thoughts are unblemished. And how, monks, is a monk one whose mental activities are calmed? Here, monks, a monk, with the abandoning of pleasure and pain, with the previous passing away of joy and grief, enters and dwells in the fourth jhāna, neither painful nor pleasant, with equanimity and mindfulness purified. Thus, monks, is a monk whose mental activities are calmed. And how, monks, is a monk one whose mind is well liberated? Here, monks, a monk’s mind is liberated from greed, liberated from hatred, liberated from delusion. Thus, monks, is a monk whose mind is well liberated. And how, monks, is a monk one whose wisdom is well liberated? Here, monks, a monk knows: ‘Greed has been abandoned in me, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made non-existent, unable to arise in the future’; knows: ‘Hatred has been abandoned in me …’; knows: ‘Delusion has been abandoned in me, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made non-existent, unable to arise in the future.’ Thus, monks, is a monk whose wisdom is well liberated. Monks, all those noble ones in the past who lived the noble dwelling — they lived these ten noble dwellings. All those noble ones in the future who will live the noble dwelling — they will live these ten noble dwellings. All those noble ones in the present who live the noble dwelling — they live these ten noble dwellings. These, monks, are the ten noble dwellings that the noble ones have lived, are living, and will live.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 10.20
📖 Read this sutta
